does: This paper empirically evaluates how Reinforcement Learning From Human Feedback (RLHF) affects trustworthiness across multiple dimensions in large language models. The authors test preference-aligned models on five trustworthiness benchmarks: toxicity, stereotypical bias, machine ethics, truthfulness, and privacy. A key finding is that RLHF trained on general-purpose human preferences does not automatically improve trustworthiness and often produces counterintuitive degradation across these verticals. To understand why, the paper adapts influence function-based data attribution methods to the RLHF setting, enabling researchers to trace how individual fine-tuning examples affect performance on specific trustworthiness benchmarks. The authors demonstrate the feasibility of this attribution approach and provide estimated scores showing which training data most influences each trustworthiness dimension.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about whether alignment methods actually produce safer and more trustworthy AI systems. It extends the influence function literature, originally developed for supervised learning, into the RLHF domain where attribution is more complex due to the reinforcement learning objective. The paper responds to implicit assumptions in the field that preference alignment universally improves model safety and reliability, questioning whether human preferences in training data inherently capture trustworthiness requirements. It connects to broader work on machine ethics evaluation, bias measurement, and LLM safety benchmarking that has highlighted tensions between different desiderata in model behavior.
enables: This work opens several research directions for improving preference alignment methods. It suggests that future RLHF procedures should explicitly optimize for multiple trustworthiness objectives simultaneously rather than assuming general preference data captures all safety concerns. The influence function attribution framework enables targeted data curation and filtering to address specific trustworthiness failures. It points toward multi-objective alignment approaches that balance cognitive performance with verifiable trustworthiness improvements, and encourages more granular investigation of what human preferences actually encode regarding safety and reliability. The methodology also enables post-hoc auditing of production models to identify which training data contributed to observed trustworthiness failures.
